speaker: interviewee
text: You have to look only on you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 3, 1996. Our survivor is Pearl Sapoznik. Her maiden name was Sztejnberg. I'm Lorrie Fein. We're in Brooklyn, New York, the United States of America, and our language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today is December 3, 1996. Our survivor is Pearl Sapoznik. Her maiden name was Sztejnberg. I'm Lorrie Fein. We're in Brooklyn, New York, the United States of America, and our language is English. Good morning. Good morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell us your name with the spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name? Pearl Sapoznik. The spelling. P-E-A-R-L S-A-- Sapoznik. S-A-P-O-Z-N-I-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pirl Sztejnberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. P-I-R-L S-Z-T-E-- it's a J. J-N-B-E-R-G. Sztejnberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No Hebrew name. Just Pirl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I went to school, they used to call me Pola.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give us your date of birth, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born June 12, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 76 proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in this town named Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-V-N-O, Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was not far from the-- from the Russian border, about 60 kilometers from the Russian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could you tell us about Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Rowne was a beautiful-- it wasn't a shtetl. It was a, a [YIDDISH], a big town. We had about 45,000 people living. Mostly Jewish people. The rest were Poles, German, Czech, Ukraine, all kinds.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Rowne look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a, a big-- like a-- like a mansion. It's a big Jewish town. You have a lot of-- a lot of schools, private schools, gymnasiums, sport clubs, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] stores. Mostly the-- the stores were-- were in Jewish stores. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What businesses do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember stores from that were selling like textiles, dishes. It has a-- you know, like a stock market, you know, that you got $1, you got to the [POLISH]. You change this for-- for-- for zlotys. It was 9-- 9-- that was a long time ago. 9 zlotys was $1. When you get a few dollars from America, you go to the [POLISH] and they change it for zlotys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any mountains or rivers in this area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was a river that's just crossing the town-- Ossetia. The river was Ossetia, yeah, a river. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the exact address of your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. I lived on 150 [POLISH] Pontio Twskego.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll-- I'll try. Pontio Twskego. P-O-N-T-I-O. Pontio. T-W-S-K-E-G-O, Pontio Twskego. I think I came close. Came close.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your house look like? What did your home look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My home? We lived in a small house, about two families. And it was a small-- an old house. Not exactly a new house. An old house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms did it have?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had about two rooms and a kitchen space.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have running water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we have to go to the pump to bring some water. And it was very, very hard because my father, he used to bring the water. Yeah. And we have like a-- a [YIDDISH], you call it in Yiddish. It's a barrel. And the barrel has to be filled up with water. So you go-- he went once in a-- you know, every-- every other day, he went to bring some fresh water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Isaac, Isaac Steinberg. My father was a self-educated man, very worldly man. He knew the history and the geography of-- of the whole world. And we really looked up to-- to my father. He was our father, our leader, our everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he get this education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Self. It was self. He was reading a lot. I remember that every week my father used to get the newspaper from Warsaw, like 'Der Moment,' the [POLISH], the and my father was reading those papers. And on a Saturday afternoon, my father had a lot of friends that used to come. My mother put some [? kichel ?] on the table, and my father was reading to them. Everybody was sitting and listening to my father. They really, really looked up to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- a house painter. It was a poor life. We had a poor life. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back to him, what memory do you have? What special one thought comes to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the one thing that-- it's really because of my father I survived, I-- I'm alive. Without my father, I would never escape. My father took us out, and he said, you're-- you're going to go now. We're going to leave this place because it was very, very bad. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Rivke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Git. G-I-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work outside of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother took care of the family. We were a family of three children. I was the oldest of three girls. My middle sister, Ruchel, and my younger sister, Pesie. And my mother took care of the children. And she went shopping, she cooked, she cleaned, she washed the clothes, she did everything. My mother never worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a special memory of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us a little bit about your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My middle sister, Ruchel, she was younger six years. No, excuse me. She was younger two years. I was two years old when my mother had Ruchel. And then we have another sister, Pesie. She was eight years younger than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember only my-- my zaide-- my mother's father. He lived with us all his life. His wife died. She was very young. She left him with five children. My mother-- four boys and my mother was the only girl. And my mother took care on the-- on the children. She raised them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my grandfather's name was Icchok. Icchok. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any special effect on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My zaide was my first teacher. I was about three years old. And you already-- I have to say brucha. And I have to say, Shema Yisrael. And then he used to put a piece of paper and put it in pencil. You know, alef, bet, gimel. And I have to-- I have to go over this with-- with a pen. My zaide was my first teacher. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages were spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first language was Yiddish. That's all. We speak only Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And outside of your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We speak-- we spoke Polish. Yeah, you know, with children, to go to school. We spoke Polish. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. At about seven years old, you go to school, to public school. [POLISH]-- that's School Number Seven, yeah, a public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from school? Do you have a favorite subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have a favorite subject. As I start getting the higher grades, I was very good in Polish history. I know the Polish history. Everything from-- from the beginning, you know, from the-- all the kings, and the queens, and the novelty, and the nobility. So that's what I remember. I was very good in-- in Polish history. And then I was good in-- like you say it, you used to say, [NON-ENGLISH]. Over here, we would say it, science-- geography, poetry. That-- that-- I was pretty good in school. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I have a lot of friends. Oh yeah, friends. I started with them to school. We all live on the same street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. My best friend was Sura Steindel. She was my best friend. We grew up together. Then I had another friend, Zisel Heutmann. Those were-- were my very good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spend time with these friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when you're young, you know, you go to their house, or they come to your house. Not much to do, like on Shabbos. Not much to do. Just go to the house, and sit, and-- and talk, and-- it's children. Have fun. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, as I got older, I was about 12. So I belonged to a Zionist organization. They have a-- a girl. She was probably a little older than me. And she made a [NON-ENGLISH] from about 10 children. And we belonged to Gordonia. Her name was-- her name was Nesia Lapides. And when I was in Israel about-- later then, about 40 years later, I met her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you recognize each other easily?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I recognized her brother. A brother-- the brother said, come. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] So I went to see her. That was something. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was about 12 years old at that time. But then as I got older, you know, you have more friends. I said, oh, why do you have to go to Gordonia? Let's go to [NON-ENGLISH]. So I became a [NON-ENGLISH]. It was also nice, you know. Every-- all the Zionist organizations was really something look forward to that. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my last organization was [NON-ENGLISH]. And that-- that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of organization was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, like not too left and not too right. Just in the middle. The [NON-ENGLISH]. Now it's called [NON-ENGLISH]. But for my time, it was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents react to your going to these different organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, they were-- yeah. I have a lot of friends. They used to come to me, like for-- they had then a sefer. They used to come to my house. I used to go to their house. And I had-- I had a very busy life. Yeah. Sort of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other religious education other than from your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had-- I had a rebbe coming to teach. And then I had a Yiddish [YIDDISH] who used to come here. I didn't go to like a beit yaakov school or a beit rivka. That was my-- that was my Jewish education, yes. I have a rebbe was teaching me davening, and then I have [YIDDISH] in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish community. Everybody was the same. We were all not rich. Everybody had the same-- it was close. It's come Shabbos, everybody go to shul. And then after shul, you come home, you have your lunch, and your family come to visit you. Especially my-- the family. My mother had four brothers, and they used to come every Shabbos and sit and talk. Reminiscing about the good old days for the Tsar, when the Tsar was-- when the Tsar was the Tsar, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the rabbi's name. No, I don't. It was a shamash and a rabbi. He-- he was doing both, both those things. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe Shabbos in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, Shabbos. You know, my father-- as-- as a young girl, I used to go with my father to shul. My father was a cohen. And I-- you know, I was about six years old. He used to take me by his hand. And I went with my father to shul. And my father, when he was duchening, I didn't-- I didn't know what is this. But then I realized that my father was-- yeah. Coming home, my mother served lunch. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What would she serve?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, mostly we have cholent. Every Shabbos, we have cholent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in the cholent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was meat, and potatoes, and beans-- mostly beans. And my mother was-- was, you know, was baking for-- every, every Shabbos has to be baked challah, and teiglach, and strudel. So I don't have too long my days to rest or it depends. Somebody came to visit. And that's how we spent Shabbos. Then we have shaloshudes. My mother made our own [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's buttermilk we used to make. Yeah. Yeah. That was, you know, typical.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked everything. I liked everything. My mother used to bake a delicious strudel from apple, apple strudel. That was very good. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help with the baking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just watched. I watched my mother when she made the chicken when she come from the shochet. I watched my mother. And I watched her baking. In the winter time, it was very hard because the-- the nights are long. So my mother used to [NON-ENGLISH] the challah, like Thursday night. And she'd get up about 3 o'clock, and she'd start to bake.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first she made the latkes. It was [NON-ENGLISH] latkes from buckwheat flour. Yeah. And were-- and some other things also. But when we got up, the children to go to school, the latkes were already on the table. We did tea. And that's what my mother was [NON-ENGLISH]. Could you describe Purim? Purim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Purim. Purim. We had a yeshiva by us, you know. The yeshiva boys used to come. They used to make like they go to the houses and they make a play. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] They come to the house, and they had a little corner, and they-- they'd perform. They perform for us. That's-- that's Purim for us. And then, you know, we had a Purim seudat. My mother used to bake a challah, a [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH]. It was like a half a table. And then she makes, you know, like-- either she makes a goose probably for the seudat. And everybody come to the Purim seudat. All the family-- especially my mother's brothers. They used to come to us with the family. Purim was-- And then as I grow older, they used to make a Purim ball from the Zionist organizations. And used to dress up in different kind of costumes, and we went out. It was-- it was something looking forward. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any costume you wore?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it once we had like in-- from-- from the [NON-ENGLISH]. They had like a play. [NON-ENGLISH] is like-- is a lover. Like a lover. [NON-ENGLISH] means love. So afterwards, dressed up with, you know, pants and with a lot of sequins, and with crowns on the head. Different kind of-- you know, the children. You know, it was really-- you have to make it your own. But I didn't know what my other friend was going to wear, so I have to be very careful. [LAUGHS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pesach. Pesach. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother worked about a month before Pesach. Everything has to be kashered. And the kitchen has to be [NON-ENGLISH]. And [NON-ENGLISH]. We didn't have a lot of the dishes, but you have to make them-- you know, kashering the dishes. The [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH]. And we helped. We helped. Yeah. Everything has to be changed, yeah, everything. We didn't have too much, but whatever we have, we have a lot of Pesadich dishes. [NON-ENGLISH] that they come-- they came from the-- from the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody has a favorite. And this was the-- the best time. The children were really looking forward helping take the dishes, and wash it, and display it. Yeah. Where did you get matzah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Matzah-- we baked matzah. It was a special place that they opened up just for baking the matzah, so-- but you don't have to bring your own flour. They bake-- you come, you order your matzah, and they bake it for you, then they bring it home. And you push it-- you push it in a-- you put it in a special place in a corner. It came in a big box, a [NON-ENGLISH]. And my mother-- this was already Pesadich. Nobody even supposed to look. And you cover it up, and then you start bringing in-- you know, the rest. Potatoes and onions. And-- and you didn't have matzah meal. You didn't have farfel. You have to do it-- you have to do it yourself. It was a thing like-- it's called a stamp. And you stamp it. And this was going on to the whole family. And then you [? sip ?] it, and you make from that farfel and matzah meal for baking. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have travelers coming to your area visiting you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Travelers? Yeah, we have family and-- in-- in smaller towns. Like we have family in Kostopol. We have family in [POLISH]. We have family in [POLISH]. They used to come to visit us once in a while, yeah. But that was short distances. It would be about an hour with the train. They used to come to visit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing before the war-- before the war actually started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war? I was-- I was already finished with school, because we all go to seven-- seven grades. It's seven-- I was about 14, 15. And then I went to learn a-- a trade. I learn how to sew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman took me in, and she showed me how to sew. Yeah. I-- I was working like a year without money, and then she start paying me. Yeah. I was about 16 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rowne, yeah. 16, 17, yeah. So that's how I know how to sew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the woman's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mirl. Mirl Shmuach. Her name was Mirl Shmuach. Yeah. She was a very special lady to me. She taught me a lot. And she had small-- two small children, a boy and a girl-- [? Zizela. ?] And the boy's name, she used to call him [? Engela. ?] She didn't call him by a name. She called them [? Zizela ?] and [? Engela. ?] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with-- not in the Polish, no. Not by the Polack, we didn't have passports. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have access to a telephone or a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any telephones. Who calls on telephone? We didn't have radios till about 1939. We didn't have radios. No. No telephone, no radios. Maybe it was a-- a telephone in the-- in the post office, but nobody called. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the government changes in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: It changes one, two, three.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Poles was-- in 1939, that was September 1. Just Erev Rosh Hashanah, the war broke out between German there in Pole-- and the-- and the Poles. So it was right-- we felt right away the-- the-- the-- the airplanes were-- right away, they were bombarding. For three, four days, we couldn't go out. Yeah. So we were go-- we went to like-- like in a suburb from Rowne. And there we stayed. But the war didn't last too long. It was about-- maybe 10 days, and Poland was destroyed. Nothing. 10, maybe 12 days. And then we were all-- then we were without-- without any government. And then for before Yom Kippur, the Russian came to us. Because the Russians were not far from the Polish-- from the Polish border.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that change?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. We didn't know what-- what Russian-- what the Soviet-- we didn't know what's what. But at least they didn't kill us. That was it. Can I have a drink? [DRINKS] They left us alone. You know, whoever. You know, they came. They help-- they helped us. Yeah. It was right away. It was a big change. It was a big change. Everything was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel? What did you sense?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't know, you know? They-- they came in with the tanks, the-- the-- the Russians. And they-- and they-- right away, they make theirselves at home. And nothing like in the first few days. We didn't have any bread, any nothing, because still it took them time to-- to start. But then, we have to wait on line for bread. You have to go out like at night. But you know, little by little, they got-- it got better. You got bread, you got sugar, but you have to wait on line. Yeah. You have to wait on line for hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any new rules or laws? What changes were there actually with the Russian government?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing much, you know. They-- you know, maybe in the-- the higher, so some, maybe they-- but not for the average person. It wasn't too much of a change. So the Russian came. They saved us. If you wouldn't be for the Russian, probably the Germans would come. And it didn't take them too long. It took them another year and a-- a year and a half. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel the presence of communism in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a change, you know. We didn't know. We saw the-- the-- the soldiers with different uniforms, with different languages, with everything different. But it was OK. They didn't kill us. Again, they did not. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a difference in the businesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They closed all the businesses. They confiscated everything. All of a sudden, all the stores-- you know, we had a lot of stores. Nothing. Everything disappeared. But it took quite a few months. They start bringing in more goods, you know, like-- but you have to wait on line. You know, necessities, not luxury. Necessities-- you need a pair of shoes, you need a pair of stockings, you know. But this-- it came, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father's work affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father was still a painter. My father was still a painter. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your work affected at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: My work? I went to work-- not already like sewing, but I went to work in a store. You know, they were supplying the-- the schools with, you know, like bakery goods. You know, the children, they should have lunch. So I worked in that place. Yeah, it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the presence of the Nazis in the other parts of-- of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were-- not too much. Not too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what reports did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell you the truth, I-- we didn't-- I didn't know. We didn't. Maybe we have a lot or-- a lot of people coming from Poland. They-- they were escaping, so they came to Rowne like [NON-ENGLISH]. So they were telling us stories what the Nazis are doing. But in Rowne, we didn't feel-- we didn't feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction to those stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very sad. They were grabbing people from the streets and then killing them. And this was-- I mean, this is our people. This is our people. This was sad, but we can do nothing. We couldn't do nothing about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think would happen to you? Did you have any ideas about the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't know. Well, the Soviet, maybe they'll-- they'll stay with us. Maybe this is it. So we make the best. But they didn't take too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] And on June 22, 1941, the German broke the pact non-aggressive with the Russians, and they-- and they invaded-- they invaded Russia. Especially our-- our place, especially Rowne. And Wolyn-- that what you call Wolyn especially. That was-- was invaded. And right away, the first-- the first day, we felt the bombs. They were bombarding like no tomorrow. Yeah. Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any awareness that this was going to happen, any warnings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have. Yeah. But my father, he read the book when Hitler wrote the book. So he know that Hitler wants to destroy the Jews. This is no-- no escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father knew about it, yeah. But we as young people, we didn't think about it. So the Russians came, we had the Russian. But we didn't think about other-- otherwise. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that day-- that was that-- like I mentioned this-- it was constantly bombarding Rowne. And Rowne was always like destroyed. The only way to go was to-- towards the Russian border. Did you see any of the Germans when they occupied?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. When we-- when we went away, we left Rowne June 25 towards the Russian-- Russian border. And two days later, the German came to Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made the arrangements for your leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have-- we didn't have an arrangement. You know, my father said, let's go. Let's go in the suburb. Let's go into the [NON-ENGLISH]. So we'll wait till the bomb, but then we'll come back. But it wasn't back. We couldn't go back because Rowne was burning from three sides. The only way was towards the Russian border. And that's what we start our journey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone leave with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my-- and my younger sister. My middle sister wasn't-- at that time when the war broke out, she wasn't in Rowne. She was working in a place. They used to ship in films from Russia, and she was examining those films. Quite a few young girls worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she worked there. And she was very good, so they gave her like to go to a-- in Russian you call this a [RUSSIAN]. Like in a hotel in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was near the-- the Romanian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went away on the-- on June 14. And on June 22, the war broke out. She couldn't come back, but she came back when the-- when the-- when the German was there. Somehow she came back. And a neighbor of ours-- she was hiding by-- by Christian people. So she told us the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop the tape now. We're going to stop.
